CURRENT AFFAIRS.

When the site of a town is owned by a number of
landlords, at least the price of land is subject to some
competition. But in the case where one landlord owns
the whole site of the town and the land surrounding it,
the state of things is desperate. Anyone who has visited
Killarney must come away convinced that it badly
needs improvement, all the more so as its slums and
squalor are emphasised by contrast with the natural
beauty of the surrounding country. At the recent con-

- ference, where an Irish Municipal and Urban Counml‘s
General Council was formed, the chairman of the Kl_]-
larney Urban Council, Mr. Tugene O’Sullivan, let in

me light on the matter. The land available for town

_ improvement at Killarney is owned by one man, the

~ Unionist and Cawtholic, Barl of Kenmare. Mr. O
livan said that the sanitary and housing conditions in
Killarney were absolutely hopeless. Tifteen years ago

 the Urban Council formulated a housing scheme, and
for an acre of otherwise useless land this noble mono-
wanted no less than £1,000! What were the

Kill

. the western world.

the site, and the arbitrators fixed the price of an acre
and a quarter at £960.

Ballyheigue in the County of Kerry has recently been
pestered with the testimonial nuisance. A few weeks
ago the hat was sent round for one Peeler Wright. He
was only in the village for & few months, and was then,
we believe on his own application, drafted to Tralee of
the sweet smelling gas. A Bung headed the
list, and many of the subscriptions were collected in
a bungery. Another testimonial was to Constable Cal-
lanan.  Two testimonials to peclers within a short space
of time is rather a heavy tax on the people of Bally-
heigue, where many of the houses or cabins badly want
to be thatched.

There was a Police Ball ai the R.I.C. Barracks in
Woodford, Co. Galway, recently. It appears the bobbies
sent out the invitations. The D.I. was there and so
was a local Justice of the Peace. Publicans’ daughters,
needless to say, were in some force at this aristocratic
function.

We read in a daily paper that at the Carlow Christ-
mas Fowl Market the prices for turkeys were—cocks,
18s. to 24s. per pair, and hens, 10s. to 12s. per pair.
What have the Suffragettes got to say to this? Surely
this inequality of the sexes should not be allowed with-
out a ‘protest. If the Suffragettes are consistent it
seems to us that they should only buy hen turkeys,
and that they should pay the same price for them as
the non-hens are fetching.

Trather C. C. Mulvihill, Vicksburg, Miss., U.S.A.,
writes under date 8rd December :— * Doubtless it will
be a surprise to you to get a letter from this portion of
It is said, however, that an Trish-
man can be found in any part of the wide world over.
I am an Irish priest, educated and ordained in All Hal-
lows. For many years I have read, aye made a study
of, the Lraner. I know I only endorse the opinions of
many when I say that I believe it has done more for
Ireland and Ireland’s cause than any other existing
paper. I do not want to lose connection with it now.
I wish to become a subscriber for 1913.”

It is hard enough to successfully establish a new
industrial firm in Ireland, and when that difficult task
is done it is not encouraging to find a difiiculty in pro-
curing workers.  Take the Kilkenny Woollen Mills
Co. that has turned the corner and has declared a divi-

.dend. The new manager, Mr. Smith, to whom by all
accounts great credit is due for the pulling up of the
business, in the course of a statement that he made at
the recent general meeting shid :—'‘ During the past
year he had gone to considerable expense in putting
in new and up-to-date machinery, but there were five
or six looms idle, as they could not possibly get young
men or young women to come forward and take posi-
tions as weayers. He did not kuow why that was so
as the wages were exceedingly good. As he said some
time ago, he wished to respect what he knew to be the
sincere feelings and desires of Captain Cuffe. . It was
his wish that all this work should be done by Irishmen
and Trishwomen, and he (speaker) was very reluctant
to import labour from across the water. S
manager had made out a return as to what the weavers
were earning for the past six weeks. He took the ¢

On S\_mduy, January 12, 1918, the annual charity
. sermon in aid of St. Mary’s Asylum, High Park, Drum-
. condra, will be preached after .the First Gospel of the
last Mass in the Church of St. Francis Xavier, Upper
Gardiner Strevt, by Father Robert Kane, S.J. On the
same day another appeal will be made after the First

Gospel of the last Mass in the Capuchin’s Church,.

Church Street, by Father Angelus, 0.8.1.C., for the
same deserving purpose. There are at present 216 in-
mates in this institution, and to maintain fhem in sick-
ness and health the Sisters have no regular fund of any
kind. Those who cannot attend the sermons may send
their donations to his Grace the Archbishop, to any of
the city clergy, or direct to the Mother Superior of the
Convent, High Park, Drumcondra, Dublin.

Next Sunday’s Orchestral Concert; (Antient Concert
Rpcms, 4 p.m.), which will be the last of the season,
_will eclipse all former concerts in point of musical ex-
cellence. The great feature of the entertainment will
be the playing of Beethoven’s great Concerto for Violin
and Orchestra. Signor Achille Simonetti will be solo
violin, and those who were so fortunate as to be pre-
sent when this great work was performed at the Or-
chestral Concert in the Gaiety Theatre will be eager to
hear it again. The items allotted to the orchestra in-
clude the Overture to ‘‘ Nozze di Figaro,”” Mozart;
““ Dans les Steppes d’Asie,” Borodin; Overture. to
“‘ Tannhauser,” Wagner, etc. Vocal items will be
contributed by Mr. Arthur McCallum.

It is bad enough electing Bungs to public boards, but
why should any board co-opt a Bung? Recently a
Bung was co-opted on the Clonmel Board of Guardians.
What was the reason for this cp-option? — Was not
‘“ the trade’” sufficiently well represenfed by that

_ patriotic publican and militant member from the Old
Bridge, Daniel F. O’Meara, T.C., P.L.G. ?

If we get Home Rule the Irish Government will have.

the appointment of the Post Office officials at once.
The Post Office officials who would have the handling

of all deposits will for certain be appointed from the -

Ancient Order of Hibernians, ‘the United Irish League
and their friends.

League Executive' to know what he has in the Post
Office Savings Bank. *

in England. = One in our possession has the Dudley
postmark. Dunmanway was inundated by these cir-
culars. One day’s batch came from Glasgow : another
big delivery came from Wolyverhampton. The lot sent
to Tarbert, Co. Kery, apparently came from Glasgow.
A circular sent to Ballymore-Iiustace, Co. Kildave,

+ Why does the Post Office circulate these things, as
being in open wrappers with ha'penny postage they
are open to Post Office inspection. Perhaps the wily
Samuel is laughing up his sleeve with satisfaction ab
the Unionists squandering their thousands of ha’pennies
in this manner.

By the way, is not the Post Office supposed to exer-

_ cise a censorship on objectionable posteards ? A Dublin

citizen sends us a posteard that was delivered at his
house addressed in unknown writing to his domestic.
He remarks :—*‘ I send you a praduction of Inglish art
which has been sent to our domestic servant here
through the British Post Office. Is there any way of
prosecuting the filthy manufacturers or publishers, or
the Post Office for being carriers of filth. Our domestic
is an innocent country girl. The writing is that of a
stranger. Responsible persons talk about the unity and
identity of interests between the democracy of Ingland
and the democracy of Ireland! Are we, a Catholic and
ancient nation, to be identified with a degenerate,
rioney-worshipping, pagan people like the English?
The national language movement is the best guard
against anglicisation, which means hell open to Ire-
land.” The card which our correspondent enclosed is
a very objectionable production. ~ We suppose there
are people who would say that it was not indecent, for
it is wonderful what quibbles people will raise on that
point. But it is nasty, degrading, low and blasphe-
mous. What is the imported Norway doing that ar-
rangements are not made to stop such things being car-
ried through the Post ?

'This is the season for giving by those who have to
those who need. From the far away island of Inish-

" bofin comes the appeal from the church in that remote

island in the Atlantic, and the appeal is one that will
specially touch the hearts of many of our readers.
There is another church, too, that has claims on the
charitable, the church in the poor parish of Ringsend,
Dublin, to which the parish priest, Father Mconey,
devotes his life. The appeal for the Penny Dinrers for
the poor given out by the Sisters of Charity at St. Taw-
rence O’Toole’s Convent, Seville Place, Dublin, is not
forgotten even in South Africa, for a reader in Cape-
town renewing his subscription to the Leaprr this weels
sent us a round sum requesting that the change be sent
to the Penny Dinners.
Homeless Poor, conducted by the Sisters of Mercy, in
_Brickfield Lane, Dublin, is a charity whose very name
speaks for it. Our Lady’s Hospice for the Dyiog in
Harold’s Cross, under the care of the Sisters of Charity,
is one of the most appealing charities in Dublin. St.
Michael’s Hospital, Kingstown, under the care of the
Sisters of Merey, appeals again (o our charitable readers
in Irish and in Bnglish. St. Mary’s Training School,
Stanhope Street, conducted by the Sisters of Charity,
where so many children are brought up and cared for,
has to, depend solely on the charity of the public as it
has no Government grant.

Bonar Law does not appear to be giving satisfaction
to the lrish Unionists. What do the English .Tories
care about Carson or ‘“ Alf Tox?'  The anti-Irish
Unionists in this country are now in their raimeis
days; and one of their delusions is that large numbers
of people in England are concerned about them. Eng-
land, of course, does not care the snap of its fingers
for the Carrion Crows once England’s own inferests run .
in a different direction. Trinity is glad enough to
sneak back to Ireland, and in a short time the anti-
Trish will tumble over one another in their anxiety to,
if we may anticipate a phrase which we will at no fa
distant date have to make current, ‘ prove their
patriotism.”” We are yet so green unc;l 80 flabby that
notwithstanding all the miserable Unionist pack has
done we suppose a lot of us will go blubbering when
the defeated rascals shed bogus patriotic tears and fall
upon our necks. The Irish Times is almost getting
cross with Bonar. It commences its leading article of
Tuesday thus:—

Irish Unionists will read Mr. Bonar Law’s
speech at Ashton-under-Lyne last night with a
deep sense of disappointment. ~We may say at
once that we had looked forward to the Unionist
leader’s statement of policy with mingled feelings
of hope and of anxiety. We have already put on
record our firm opinion of the effect which the
Marquis of Lansdowne’s withdrawal of the refer-
endwm pledge on Tariff Reform must have on the
prospects of the Unionist Party. That with-
drawal, in our belief, made dangerously uncertain
the promise, otherwise assured, of the party’s re-
turn to power at the next General Election. Since
that time nothing has occurred to shake our con-
viction, but many things have happened which
establish it.

We had hoped, therefore, that Mr. Bonar Law
would be induced to modify the Unionist Party’s
“attitude. Such hopes are finally destroyed by his

As poor Bonar Law has many things eclse to trouble
him, of more importance to him than the cause of the
Carrion Crows, Arnott’s Times is in a blue funk. It
even—notwithstanding the wooden weapons of Porta-
down—goes into a rhapsody of raimeis by talking of
civil war!  That war will be very civil, ‘“ Alf,”’ eh?
Poor Bonar is cruelly reminded of his visit to Ulster.
“CAILT says i —

Mr. Bonar Law's visit to Unionist Ulster must
still remain a vivid recollection with him. He has
had the opportunity, and he took full advantage
of the opportunity, of gauging the temper of the
northern province. Mr. Bonar Law knows, as
every sane politician of any party knows, what will
happen in Ulster if an attempt is made to put this
Home Rule Bill into force.  He has told the
Government across the floor of the House of Com-
mons that the inevitable result of such an attempt
must be, and will be, civil war. That, as we have
said again and again, is the dominating fact of the
political situation.

That is raimeis pure and simple, and it is a consola-
tion to us who have had to deplore the results of the
widespread existence of raimeis amongst our own
green Nationalists, it is a consolation to us to see the
enemies of Ireland within its gates resorting to raimeis
to keep up a semblance of courage. The wooden rifles
and the sweated operatives of Belfast make bad fight-
ing material, ““Alf,”” and many Belfast travellers and
houses have had enough of the Belfast atrocities; and
civil war even with commercial orders is not altogether
an exhilarating sport.

We see that the National Education Commissioners
are advertising for a Junior Inspector of National
schools. “‘ A knowledge of the Irish language will be
compulsory in: the case of this appointment!”’ When
will the normal state come about when the fact that a
knowledge of Irish is necessary for an inspector will
be a matter of course and not a matter for comment ?

rebels and a nation with a superabundant sense of

quence of this was that Ireland was being more or less

stances, as inexorable as the law of gravitation. Now

CERISTMAS THOUGHTS.

S it not wonderful when one comes to think of it,
this universal bowing down of a haughty world to

a Babe, even though that Babe be a hidden God?
Voltaires and Rousseaus have arisen and may yet arise,
science has trained her heaviest guns and subtlest en-
gines of warfare on the citadel where this belief in the
story of Bethlehem sits enthroned in the hearts of men,
but the golden legend, ‘‘ Peace on earth, good will to
men,’”’ first repeated by angel lips, has in it an immor-
tality not of earth and must for ever flash along our
December skies in spite of all earthly assaults what-
ever.
Christmas has various aspects. We ordinarily pic-
ture the Redeemer of mankind in His majesty—omni-
potent, omniscient, all merciful ; but on this great feast
He comes closer to earth than at any other time, He
is the Babe of Bethlehem, absolutely helpless in His
infant hours as we all have been. Hence it is fitting,
perhaps, that Christmas should be above all things
else the children’s feast. Too happily heedless to heed
the morrow, having no past to look back to, they—as
we all did once—enjoy the happy Christmas time with
absolutely nothing to mar their youthful joy.
And returning Spring that renews all nature is not
more true or beautiful than the Christmas picture that
is yearly painted on thé faces of happy childhood : and
the illusions of that happy time—the Daddy Christmas
that plods over the mountain laden with all good things,
the Santa Claus that, disdaining the orthodox mode of
entry, comes down the chimney with precious gifts for
little boys and girls—are after all among the greatest
and best realities in life. Alas that they should be so

fleeting ! Alas that not all should thus fare, tHat there
are waifs in our city stteets, homeless and frie'n‘(l\les'sr
aliens to Christian charity in a Christian land, spurnéd
from rich men’s doors as He was spurned in the Judean
hamlet nineteen centuries ago.

But for us who have left our childhood’s days behind,
no matter how much we may abandon ourselves to the
spirit of the moment, amidst all its outward revelty,
Christmas speaks to us in a solemn undertone. It is
another milestone on life’s road, and the perspective
through which we look back to our happiest days and
our happy childhood home is lengthening. Then we
all in some measure live again the good old days, and
call up visions of sacred forms long vanished, not iif a
spirit of sadness, but in a wistfulness and spirit of faith
begotten of the time, that somewhere beyond there is a
Christmas where we and all we have loved shall fore-
gather. Deride sentiment as we will, hold unrelaxed
the stern features with which we daily front the cold
world, this reminiscent mood at Christmas time is com-
mon to most of us, if not to all, and is part of its higher
joys. Nor need we fear that each recurring milestone
will find our memories growing dim and the visions of
our first remembered Christmases less palpable: in the
noon-day of life, when we are busy with the world and
its fretful cares, they may suffer a partial eclipse, but
they tell us that ever as the shadows lengthen our
vision becomes more clear, and we live nearer to our
childhood in extreme old age than in our early man-
hood.

That is one aspect of our maturer Christmases: in
its more sacred forms to be spoken of with reverence
in our family. circles round the hearth-fires that have
gleamed on our infant faces and that for ever gleam
in our exiles’ dreams; or lovingly clasped in the temple
of each man’s inner consciousness which none but oure
selves may enter or share. The poet tells us, ‘ The
sweetest songs are those that tell of saddest thought'” ;
and thus ‘mid the gayest revelry does Christmas send
in its appeal to our higher nature. We call up the
ghosts of the past—ghosts that assume shapes of fami-
liar and beloved forms long mouldering in the dust;
ghosts, alas! that also assume shapes of lost opportu-
nities and deeds of mercy and acts of kindness left
undone. :
Christmas is for most of us a glad respite from exact-
ing and monotonous toil : for this alone we bless it; and
the world would be much more drab did not this yearly
recurring festival come round to colour, to brighten,
and to soften the hard asperibies of life.. Many sad
and gloomy Christmases -this land has known, when °
the-only rays that brightened men’s lives came from
above or not at all. This perhaps is why in a world
and in an age grown drunk with matetial pleasure, we
happily still retain so much of the spiritual side of this
great festival, so many of the beautiful customs that

The fires that nightly light the Black Country may
have more of hell than heaven in their radiance, but
the lights that make the homes of rural Ireland one
universal shrine on Christmas night point wholly hea-
venwards.  They have burned through all the lc_mg
night of our history that is past: will they burn’ as
brightly when that night is a memory, and we gradually
emerge into a bright and brighter day ? 2

Tt Chrisbmas has its joys it brings its duties too. As
a nation we are proverbially not lacking in faith, but d‘o
we grasp fully enough in its practical sense the season’s
gospel of ““ Peace on earth, Goob WILL o men?’’ Have
we even enough of-that broad humanitarianism thgb
the writings of Dickens have done so much to evoke in
England ? Do we out of our abundance or over-abun-
dance, out of our poverty even, seck out our less
fortunate brethren, those who in their circumstances
are most like the Christ Babe of Bethlehem, and seek
to add something to their Christmas joys? T know
not; but I hope with all our boasted virtues we shall
not deserve the reply: ‘“ As long as you did it not to
one of these least neither did you do it to Me.””

Tt is a time of peace this Christmas time, a time of
calm and rest, when business affairs and politics and
things that vex men’s souls are rigidly shut out, yet
well may we spare a’thought and a prayer for our coun-
try whose Christmas sky at last is full of hope; whose
Christmas board too often in the past was for the
noblest of her sons the prison cell and the scaffold.
And if this Christmas sun dawns on a land greatly im-
proved and greatly hopeful of the motrow, it is because
we reap where they have sown, and have no need to
light our bivouac fires along the lines of the cholera- .
stricken Chatalja.

" ““If they lost Ulster,”” said Mr. Goschen, ‘ they

be their milch cow.””

“The real reason,”’ says the Spectator (August

taxes on. She is the milch cow of Home Rule.”

At this season of the year all are moved to relieve
by charity and benevolence the wants of their fellow-
creatures, in imitation of their Heavenly Tather, Who
at this season gave to the world the greatest gift in the
person of the Redeemer.

In His Name the Sisters of Charity appeal to their
benefactors and friends to aid them in the, relief of the

- Poor, and to help them to save the Orphan and Desti-

tute Child, by allowing them a portion of the alms
given at this season of Christmas.

There are at present 110 children in the Institution
to be supported by charitable contributions and their
own efforts. As will be remembered, St. Mary’s Train-
ing School, receiving no grant from Government, en-
tirely depends on the generosity of the Irish pecople,
and the citizens of Dublin in particular, who may look
with pride on their work of charity, as it is most credit-
able both in its workings and its results. The Sisters
of Charity, then, humbly hope that they will not be
forgotten in the Christmas contributions, and that the
little ones under their care may be able to share in the
joys of Christmas and offer their innocent prayers to
God for their generous benefactors.

Cheques and Post Office Orders are made payable to

THE RECTRESS,
Stanhope Street Convent, Dublin.

The Sisters of Charity in charge of the Hospice beg
with all earnestness to lay the claims of the dying
before the public eye and heart.

The dying! How powerfully that word has evoked
the best sympathies and most generous endeavours of
men- ever since Christianity first shed its heavenly
influence upon earth. Human pity, exalted by Divine
charity, bent like the good Samaritan over the prostrate
form of suffering in its final throes, and it does so
still—hence the establishment of a special Institution
to meet its inspiration and its needs.

Religious differences are  no impediment to
admission. The Hospice is open to all conditions and
denominations, and may, therefore, solicit the kindly
and liberal support of all. .

Every benefactor has a share in the good work, and
assists in keeping open the gate through which it is
the desira of manv a voor sufferer to pass.

Tarnestly appeal for a share in your Christmas
Alms in aid of the Sick and Destitute Poor
visited by them.

CHRISTMAS APPEALS.

Christmas Month.

5t. Michael’s Hospital,

Kingstown.

A bright Christmas all look forward to, for brightness was:
brought from Heaven by the little Infant of Bethlchem.

Wealth can purchase much that is cheerful, and can diffuse its
rays to enlighten the gloom of sickness and want.

An Irish memorial of Irish generosity, raised by the Benevolent,
supported by the Charitable Friends of the Poor, St. Michael's
ospital affords to the weary sufferers the solace and relief which
they need more at Christmas than at any othor time, as they are
debarred from the little home festivities which even the most
destitute can procure at that open-handed, open-pursed season.

Tho harbour, the railway, the bicycle, the tramway, the city,
as well as the surrounding districts, send patients, who, from
accident or ill-health, receive in St. Michael's Hospital the medical
treatment, nursing, and nourishment they so much nced when
stricken down. .

To keep the Wards, Dispensaries, Operation Theatre, etc.,
properly equipped; to store daily the quantity of Food and:
Medicine necessary; to repair hourly the wear and tear of time;
to furnish coal, gas, etc.—all this entails an expenditure which
would appear exorbitant to one who had not practical experience
of outlay, but which, nevertheless, demands constantly well-
ciphered cheques (o defray. The Sisters of Mercy, therefore, con-
fidently appeal to the proverbial liberality of the benefactors of
the suffering poor to give them such help as will enable them to
continue this great work of mercy. They are deeply grateful to
all who have perseveringly and cordially supported St. Michael's
Hospital since its opening, and whose hundredfold rewards will be
bestowed hero and hereafter by the Almighty Good God, Who
has never been outdone in generosity.

Donations will be thankfully received by His Grace the Most
Rev. Dr. Walsh, Archbishop of Dublin; by the Very Rev. Wm

J. Mu , P.P.; by the Parochial Clergy of Kingstown and the
adjoin Parishes; by the Sisters of Mercy, Baggot Street,
Dublin; and at St. Michael's Hospital, Kingstown, Ireland.
